recommended_solution: S5: Rollback deployment 2026.03.17-2 and S1: Fix session leak in rebalance_service.py
rationale: Rolling back to a known-good state (S5) immediately stops new leaks, and then applying the code fix (S1) addresses the root cause. This approach minimizes risk and downtime while ensuring a permanent resolution.

pre_checks.action: Verify current deployment version and identify previous artifact (2026.03.17-1) for rollback
pre_checks.expected_result: Confirmation of current deployment version and identification of previous artifact

pre_checks.action: Check Postgres max_connections and available connection slots
pre_checks.expected_result: Knowledge of current Postgres connection limits and available headroom

pre_checks.action: Review recent changes to the rebalance workflow and session lifecycle in the rebalance_service.py file
pre_checks.expected_result: Understanding of recent code changes and potential causes of the session leak

remediation_steps.action: Trigger rollback to deployment 2026.03.17-1 via CI/CD or manually swap the application bundle
remediation_steps.notes: This step reverts the application to a known-good state, stopping new leaks
remediation_steps.validation: Verify that the rollback is successful and the application is running with the previous version

remediation_steps.action: Restart gunicorn workers: `systemctl restart gunicorn` or equivalent
remediation_steps.notes: This step ensures that the rolled-back application is fully restarted
remediation_steps.validation: Confirm that gunicorn workers are restarted and the application is responding

remediation_steps.action: Locate portfolio/rebalance_service.py line 118 and replace bare `session = SessionLocal()` with `with SessionLocal() as session:`
remediation_steps.notes: This step applies the code fix to address the session leak
remediation_steps.validation: Verify that the code change is correctly applied and the session leak is fixed

remediation_steps.action: Deploy the code fix (deployment version 2026.03.17-3 or hotfix branch)
remediation_steps.notes: This step ensures that the code fix is deployed to the production environment
remediation_steps.validation: Confirm that the deployment is successful and the application is running with the updated code

post_fix_validation.command_or_method: Monitor pool metrics using a tool like Prometheus or Grafana
post_fix_validation.pass_criteria: Confirmed stabilization of checked_out connections and no further errors

post_fix_validation.command_or_method: Review application logs for any errors or warnings related to database connections
post_fix_validation.pass_criteria: No errors or warnings related to database connections

post_fix_validation.command_or_method: Verify available Postgres connection slots using `SHOW max_connections;` in psql

rollback_plan.trigger: If the remediation steps cause further issues or do not resolve the connection pool exhaustion
steps: Revert the code change and redeploy the previous version
Revert any changes made to Postgres configuration

parallel_mitigations: Increase Postgres max_connections and tune superuser reservation (S4) to prevent Postgres-side rejection
Temporarily disable the rebalance endpoint or rate-limit it (S3) to stop new connections from being leaked
escalation: If the remediation steps do not resolve the issue within 2 hours, escalate to the on-call DBA and/or the development team lead
communication_template: Database connection pool exhaustion incident: current status is [insert current status], expected resolution time is [insert expected resolution time]
long_term_recommendations: Regularly review and monitor database connection pool metrics
Implement automated monitoring and alerting for database connection pool issues
Consider increasing the Postgres max_connections limit and tuning superuser reservation
